summary: Jesse Daniel Ames was the driving force behind the ASWPL, essentially running it with the help of about half a dozen committed women. She was a strong, sometimes difficult personality who believed women worked differently from men and insisted on keeping women’s organizations separate from male-led groups. Ames had a clear vision that lynchings did not protect Southern white women, a stance she could confidently take after the research done at Tuskegee Institute. She was ambitious, even hoping to take over the Interracial Commission at one point, and though not always well-liked personally, she commanded loyalty and admiration from the women she led.

The ASWPL was an organization largely shaped and led by Jesse Daniel Ames with a small core group of about six women who were very active and responsive. It reached out extensively to women’s missionary societies across the South, especially involving Methodist women, and focused on the message that lynchings did not protect Southern white women. The group held annual meetings where complex issues like mob violence versus legal lynchings were discussed, and it operated fairly autonomously from the Interracial Commission. While it claimed a wide membership, the real work was done by a committed few who responded to calls and participated actively in the cause.

Alexander was the head of the Interracial Commission and allowed me considerable autonomy in my work, including how I interacted with the ASWPL. He trusted me to take charge of programs without micromanagement and was somewhat involved in the Commission’s activities, though the ASWPL operated fairly separately. I had closer contact with Alexander than Jesse Daniel Ames did, partly because of Ames’s pushy style. When Alexander went to Washington, leadership was somewhat unclear, but I remained closely involved with the Commission’s work during that time.

Mr. Eleazer was involved with the Interracial Commission to a lesser extent than Alexander but still contributed occasionally to the ASWPL’s activities. His role was more peripheral, and he was not as directly engaged in the day-to-day operations or leadership as Alexander or Jesse Daniel Ames. Nonetheless, he was part of the broader network supporting the Commission’s work during the period I was involved.

Tuskegee Institute played a crucial role in providing the solid research foundation that allowed Jesse Daniel Ames and the ASWPL to confidently assert that lynchings did not protect Southern white women. The case studies and data gathered there were instrumental in shaping the organization’s message and defending it against criticism. This research gave the ASWPL the security to focus on their core point without fear of being dismissed or attacked for ignorance.

Atlanta was a central location where the ASWPL held meetings and where many of the committed women, including Jesse Daniel Ames and her core group, were based or gathered. The city served as a hub for organizing and responding to calls related to the anti-lynching work. While not all active members lived in Atlanta, it was a key place for the group’s activities and coordination.

The Methodist Church, particularly its women’s missionary association, formed the core constituency of the ASWPL. Jesse Daniel Ames was able to mobilize these women effectively, as they were already organized and motivated around similar goals. This connection gave the ASWPL a strong base of support and helped sustain its activities across the South. The Methodist women’s involvement was central to the organization’s success in pushing its anti-lynching message.
